title: Shape of sampling distributions for differences in sample means
description: Practice using the Central limit theorem to determine when sampling distributions for differences in sample means are approximately normal.
topic_path: Sampling distributions for differences in sample means
Shape of sampling distributions for differences in sample means

assessment_items.question_content:
Gabi and Mina both play a single-player game where they try to survive as many rounds as possible without crashing their car into a wall. Here's the historical data for how many rounds each player survives in a single attempt at this game:

Player | Shape | Mean | Standard deviation
- | - | -: | -:
Gabi | Normal | $35.2$ | $7.1$
Mina | Normal | $30.3$ | $8.6$

They decide to each play $10$ attempts and calculate the sample mean of how many rounds they survived. They will then look at the difference in their sample means $\left( \bar{x}_\text{G} - \bar{x}_\text{M} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{G} - \bar{x}_\text{M}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The table tells us that both populations are normal. So the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{G} - \bar{x}_\text{M}$ will be exactly normal (it doesn't matter that both the sample sizes are less than $30$).

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{G} - \bar{x}_\text{M}$ will be exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's not normal, because the populations aren't normal, and both sample sizes are less than $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Xavi wants to measure the impact of using water for irrigation on a stream's flow rate. The flow rates both upstream and downstream of the irrigation point are normally distributed.

For each location, Xavi selects a random time each month and measures the flow rate in liters per second. After he has $12$ samples per location, he will look at the difference $\left( \text{upstream} - \text{downstream} \right)$ in their sample means.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{U} - \bar{x}_\text{D}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

We already know that both populations are normal. So the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{U} - \bar{x}_\text{D}$ will be exactly normal (it doesn't matter that both the sample sizes are less than $30$).

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{U} - \bar{x}_\text{D}$ will be exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's not normal, because the populations aren't normal, and both sample sizes are less than $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
A [[☃ definition 1]] shop tries to give consistent scoop sizes, but some flavors of gelato are denser than others. Here's the historical data for the mass per scoop of two flavors:

Flavor | Shape | Mean $(\text{g})$ | Standard deviation $(\text{g})$
- | - | -: | -:
Hazelnut | Normal | $83$ | $9.3$
Dulce de leche | Normal | $67.5$ | $7.8$

Suppose they weigh a sample of $20$ randomly selected scoops of hazelnut gelato and $15$ scoops of dulce de leche gelato. They will then look at the difference in their sample means $\left( \bar{x}_\text{H} - \bar{x}_\text{D} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{H} - \bar{x}_\text{D}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The table tells us that both populations are normal. So the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{H} - \bar{x}_\text{D}$ will be exactly normal (it doesn't matter that both the sample sizes are less than $30$).

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{H} - \bar{x}_\text{D}$ will be exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company has offices in two different countries. Suppose that the average age (in years) of the employees at location A is $40.1$ with a standard deviation of $5.5$, and the average at location B is $36.7$ with a standard deviation of $6.3$.

Every month, the company takes separate random samples of $50$ employees from each location for a survey. Each time, they look at the difference in the mean age sampled from each location $\left( \bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem doesn't mention the shape of either population. But both sample sizes are large $(50\geq30)$. So the sampling distribution of both $\bar{x}_\text{A}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{B}$ will be approximately normal, and the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$ will also be approximately normal.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$ will be approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's not normal, because the populations aren't normal, and both sample sizes are less than $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here is a summary of the number of errors that people made while taking the driving test at a testing center.

Took a driving class? | Mean | Standard deviation | Number of test takers
- | - | -: | -: | -:
Yes | $5.6$ | $3.8$ | $2{,}650$
No | $8.2$ | $4.4$ | $1{,}070$

Suppose that the administrators of the testing center take a stratified random sample of $60$ people who took a driving class and $40$ people who did not, then look at the difference in the mean number of errors between the samples $\left( \bar{x}_\text{Y} - \bar{x}_\text{N} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{Y} - \bar{x}_\text{N}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem doesn't mention the shape of either population. But both sample sizes are large $(60 > 40 \geq30)$. So the sampling distribution of both $\bar{x}_\text{Y}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{N}$ will be approximately normal, and the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{Y} - \bar{x}_\text{N}$ will also be approximately normal.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{Y} - \bar{x}_\text{N}$ will be approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Here is a summary of the Boston Marathon completion times for two years.

Year | Mean (minutes) | Standard deviation (minutes) | Number of runners
- | -: | -: | -: | -:
2012 | $263$ | $50$ | $21{,}576$
2014 | $243$ | $52$ | $31{,}984$

A sports journalist takes separate random samples of $35$ runners from each year. Then they look at the difference in the mean completion time sampled from each year $\left( \bar{x}_\text{2012} - \bar{x}_\text{2014} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{2012} - \bar{x}_\text{2014}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem doesn't mention the shape of either population. But both sample sizes are large $(35 \geq30)$. So the sampling distribution of both $\bar{x}_\text{2012}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{2014}$ will be approximately normal, and the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{2012} - \bar{x}_\text{2014}$ will also be approximately normal.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{2012} - \bar{x}_\text{2014}$ will be approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company has offices in two different countries. Employee salaries at both locations are strongly skewed toward the higher salaries.

Every month, the company takes separate random samples of $60$ employees from each location for a survey. Each time, they look at the difference in the mean salary sampled from each location $\left( \bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem tells us that salaries at both locations are skewed. But both sample sizes are large $(60\geq30)$. So the sampling distribution of both $\bar{x}_\text{A}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{B}$ will be approximately normal, and the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$ will also be approximately normal.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$ will be approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's skewed, because the populations are skewed, and both sample sizes are at least $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
A bakery records whether the customer is a loyalty program member or not on each receipt. Total purchase price for both members and non-members is skewed toward the higher prices.

The bakery takes separate random samples of $45$ receipts from members and non-members. They look at the difference in the mean total price from each group $\left( \text{member} - \text{non-member} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{M} - \bar{x}_\text{N}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem tells us that the total prices for both groups are skewed. But both sample sizes are large $(45\geq30)$. So the sampling distribution of both $\bar{x}_\text{M}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{N}$ will be approximately normal, and the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{M} - \bar{x}_\text{N}$ will also be approximately normal.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{M} - \bar{x}_\text{N}$ will be approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's skewed, because the populations are skewed, and both sample sizes are at least $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a game, one team member draws pictures to represent a word while the other team members have $60$ seconds to guess the word. The following table summarizes the time it takes for team members to successfully guess the word.

Word category | Shape | Mean (seconds) | Standard deviation (seconds)
- | - | -: | -:
Action | Left-skewed | $51.0$ | $13.7$
Object | Left-skewed | $42.8$ | $11.9$

Suppose the game designers take a random sample of $75$ times when a team member was drawing an action word and $100$ times when a team member was drawing an object.  Then they look at the difference in the mean guessing time sampled from each category $\left( \bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{O} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{O}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem tells us that guessing times for both word categories are skewed. But both sample sizes are large $(100 > 75 \geq30)$. So the sampling distribution of both $\bar{x}_\text{A}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{O}$ will be approximately normal, and the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{O}$ will also be approximately normal.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{O}$ will be approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's skewed, because the populations are skewed, and both sample sizes are at least $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company has offices in two different countries. Employee commute times at both locations are skewed toward longer commutes.

Every month, the company takes separate random samples of $10$ employees from each location for a survey. Each time, they look at the difference in the mean commute time sampled from each location $\left( \bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem tells us that commute times at both locations are skewed, and both sample sizes are small $(10<30)$, so neither the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A}$ nor of $\bar{x}_\text{B}$ will be approximately normal. The sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$ won't be normal either.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B}$ is not normal, because the populations are skewed, and both sample sizes are less than $30$.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's not normal, because the populations are skewed, and both sample sizes are less than $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
A reading society allows its members to request books, then pick up their books at the library or have them delivered to their home. The society leaders randomly select $24$ members who pick up their books at the library and $12$ members who get their books at home. They will then calculate the mean number of books checked out per year in each sample, and look at the difference between those sample means $\left( \bar{x}_\text{L} - \bar{x}_\text{H} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{L} - \bar{x}_\text{H}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem doesn't mention the shape of either population, and both sample sizes are small $(12<24<30)$, so we don't know the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{L}$ nor of $\bar{x}_\text{H}$. So, we don't know the shape of the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{L} - \bar{x}_\text{H}$ either.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{L} - \bar{x}_\text{H}$ cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's not normal, because the populations are skewed, and both sample sizes are less than $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kody wants to test to be sure his soap dispenser design works fairly for people with different skin colors. He selects separate random samples of $25$ workers with light skin and $25$ with dark skin. For each worker, he measures the maximum distance from the dispenser at which it detects the employee's hand. Then he looks at the difference in the sample means $\left( \bar{x}_\text{Light} - \bar{x}_\text{Dark} \right)$.

**What do we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{Light} - \bar{x}_\text{Dark}$, and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Thinking about population shapes and sample sizes

What we know about the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ depends on the shape of each population distribution from which we sample, and our sample sizes.

* If **both populations are normal**, then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **exactly normal** regardless of sample sizes.
* If one or both populations are **not normal** (or their shapes are unknown), then the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2$ is **approximately normal** as long as our sample size is at least $30$ from the not-normal population(s).

What do we know about the population shapes and the sample sizes in this scenario?

The problem doesn't mention the shape of either population, and both sample sizes are small $(25<30)$, so we don't know the shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{Light}$ nor of $\bar{x}_\text{Dark}$. So, we don't know the shape of the sampling distribution of their difference $\bar{x}_\text{Light} - \bar{x}_\text{Dark}$ either.

###Answer

The shape of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{Light} - \bar{x}_\text{Dark}$ cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.

choices.content: It's exactly normal, because both populations are normally distributed.
It's approximately normal, because both sample sizes are at least $30$.
It's not normal, because the populations are skewed, and both sample sizes are less than $30$.
The shape cannot be determined since we don't know the shape of either population distribution.
